description: Clean baseline for the solver3 objective autoresearch lane after the x100 metric-scale reset; per-case score metrics are the main anti-gaming signal.

Adaptive temperature calibration: catastrophically bad. Calibrated T_initial too high (~55 for balance cases), making search random. Also surfaced deeper bug: cooling schedule uses max_iterations=3M but only ~188K iterations complete in 15s, so temperature barely changes.

asi.hypothesis: Calibrate T_initial from positive delta samples to give 40% uphill acceptance
asi.rollback_reason: Primary 186.62 vs baseline 107.85. T_initial calibrated to ~55, T_final=1.375 → search never converges.
asi.root_cause_discovered: Cooling schedule mismatch: max_iterations=3M but only ~188K iterations in 15s → temperature stays near 2.0 throughout. T_initial=2.0 too low for balance penalties but adaptive calibration overshoots.
asi.next_action_hint: Switch to TIME-BASED cooling (elapsed/time_limit as progress) when time_limit is set. Keep T_initial=2.0, T_final=0.05 but let cooling happen in wall-clock space. This alone won't help balance case but fixes the cooling mismatch first.

description: Time-based cooling: max(iter_fraction, time_fraction) as SA progress. Fixes the schedule mismatch where max_iterations=3M but only ~188K iters complete in 15s. attribute_balance 1.404→0.995 (beats reference), synthetic_partial below reference too. Also 3x raw iteration speedup from debouncing Instant::elapsed every 64 iters.

asi.hypothesis: max(iter_progress, time_progress) drives SA cooling so whichever stop-condition binds ensures T reaches T_final
asi.key_finding: Iteration rate ~188K/15s vs max_iterations=3M meant T barely moved (stayed at 1.64 throughout). With time-fraction dominating, T now properly cools 2.0→0.05 over the budget. attribute_balance 1.404→0.995, primary 107.85→100.11
asi.speedup: Debouncing Instant::elapsed every 64 iters cut solver3_raw_score_us from 79.6 to 24.7 (3x)
asi.remaining_gaps: large_gender 1.015x, sailing_trip 1.017x — both within 2% of reference

description: T_initial=1.0, T_final=0.02: cooler start hurt sailing_trip (1.017→1.034) and large_gender (1.015→1.025). Sailing fixed-iteration also regressed to 1.089, confirming T=1.0 is too cold for cases that need exploration. Previous T=2.0 was better.

asi.hypothesis: T_initial=1.0, T_final=0.02 reduces random hot-phase exploration
asi.rollback_reason: 100.17 vs prev 100.11. sailing_trip 1.017→1.034, large_gender 1.015→1.025. Fixed-iteration sailing 1.089 confirms too cold. Cases needing escape from local optima need T≥2.0.
asi.next_action_hint: T_initial=2.0, T_final=0.05 is the best known. Try population-based restart or reheating when no_improvement > threshold to improve sailing/gender gaps within the existing T schedule.

description: Two-cycle incumbent restart (midpoint warm restart from best-so-far) regressed overall. It helped sailing_trip (1.017→0.992) but hurt attribute_balance (0.995→1.045) and fixed-iteration quality. The restart family is still promising, but unconditional midpoint restart is too disruptive.

asi.hypothesis: A second full cooling cycle from the incumbent best state can escape late local minima better than one long SA pass
asi.rollback_reason: Primary regressed 100.11→100.95. Attribute-balance and fixed-iteration quality both worsened materially. Unconditional midpoint restart is too blunt.
asi.mechanism_family_signal: Still promising as a restart/perturbation family because sailing_trip improved below reference while other cases regressed. Needs selective triggering, not mandatory midpoint restart on every case.
asi.next_action_hint: Incubation follow-up: make restart conditional on stagnation/no-improvement and only when current state has drifted meaningfully from best. Avoid restarting cases already converging well.

description: Conditional stagnation restart is much better than unconditional midpoint restart: fixed-iteration recovered to 100.0 and sailing stayed below reference, but primary still regressed vs best (100.11→100.23) because attribute_balance slipped from 0.995 to 1.015. Restart family still plausible with stricter trigger.

asi.hypothesis: Restart from incumbent-best only under stagnation can help hard local-minimum cases without forcing all cases through a second cooling cycle
asi.rollback_reason: Still worse than best: 100.11→100.23, mainly due to slight attribute_balance regression. Restart trigger is still too permissive.
asi.mechanism_family_signal: Directionally better than unconditional restart (100.95→100.23), so the family is not dead yet. Sailing and synthetic remain good; stricter trigger may preserve attribute_balance.
asi.next_action_hint: Final incubation follow-up: require larger drift-from-best and later progress window before restart, so only truly stuck runs reheat. If that still misses, retire restart family for now and move to a new neighborhood/move type.

description: Final restart-family incubation attempt with much stricter trigger still regressed (100.60). It preserved attribute_balance/fixed-iteration but lost the sailing benefit and slowed hotpaths badly. Restart-based perturbation is retired for now; next experiments should pivot to a genuinely new neighborhood/move type.

asi.hypothesis: A very strict stagnation-triggered incumbent restart might preserve already-good cases while still helping rare local-minimum cases
asi.rollback_reason: Primary 100.60 vs best 100.11. Sailing benefit disappeared while hotpath and runtime diagnostics degraded materially. Restart-family ROI is poor.
asi.mechanism_family_signal: After three variants, restart/perturbation does not look like the next best path for this lane. It can be revisited later, but should be deprioritized now.
asi.next_action_hint: Pivot to a true search-innovation experiment: implement a new compound move / neighborhood rather than more restart tuning.

description: Tried a richer best-of-k sampled neighborhood inside existing move families (k=3 for swap/transfer, k=1 for clique). This enlarged the local neighborhood and helped sailing, but regressed the primary fixed-time aggregate versus the 100.105 best due to runtime inflation (~48us raw score vs ~24.7 baseline) and losses on large_gender/social_golfer/synthetic.

asi.hypothesis: A small best-of-k random neighborhood inside swap/transfer would convert recent speed headroom into better local move quality and improve the fixed-time solver3 objective suite.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric regressed from the best kept run and hotpath/runtime cost roughly doubled, so the richer neighborhood was too expensive at k=3.
asi.next_action_hint: Incubate the neighborhood family in a cheaper form: try asymmetric draws (e.g. swap=2, transfer=1), selective best-of-k only when temperature is low or for one family at a time, or targeted candidate generation rather than brute extra previews.
observations: Fixed-time objective suite score 100.2793 was worse than the best kept 100.1055.
Sailing improved to 1.062, but large_gender fell to 0.985 and social_golfer/synthetic also slipped.
solver3_raw_score_us increased to 48.04, suggesting preview multiplication dominated the benefit.

description: Incubated the richer-neighborhood family with a cheap asymmetric variant: best-of-2 on swap only, transfer/clique unchanged. This still regressed the primary metric badly, driven by a huge sailing collapse, so greedy local preview selection appears harmful for this family even when the runtime overhead is moderated.

asi.hypothesis: A cheaper swap-only best-of-k neighborhood might retain the upside of richer local choice without the runtime inflation of sampling multiple transfer previews.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric worsened sharply to 112.6265, with sailing exploding to 1.8003 fixed-time and 1.9072 fixed-iteration, so the neighborhood family appears directionally wrong rather than merely too expensive.
asi.next_action_hint: Retire naïve best-of-k preview selection for now. If revisiting neighborhood enrichment later, make it structurally different: target specific conflict patterns, add diversity/randomization guards, or score compound moves rather than greedily picking the locally best sampled swap.
observations: Swap-only best-of-2 still increased solver3_raw_score_us to 34.15, but the main failure was search quality, not just runtime.
The damage concentrated in stretch_sailing_trip_demo_real while most other cases stayed near baseline.
This suggests local delta-greediness can steer solver3 into a bad basin on sailing-like instances.

description: Tried a lightweight exact-undo tabu memory for swap and transfer moves, blocking recent reverse moves while leaving clique swaps unchanged. This was a genuine metaheuristic change, but it still regressed the primary fixed-time aggregate versus the 100.105 best and increased solver overhead materially.

asi.hypothesis: A tiny tabu memory that blocks immediate reverse swap/transfer moves would reduce cycling and let solver3 spend more time exploring new basins, improving fixed-time objective quality.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric worsened to 101.0031 and runtime overhead rose notably, so exact-undo tabu did not pay for itself.
asi.next_action_hint: Retire simple reverse-move tabu for now. If revisiting search memory later, make it more state-aware: target repeated group-pair churn or repeated person-pair contact regressions rather than blanket move reversal bans.
observations: Large_gender, sailing, and synthetic all improved over reference, but not enough to beat the best kept aggregate because social_golfer slipped and runtime cost increased.
solver3_raw_score_us rose to 39.82 and clique/transfer path timings also increased, so even lightweight policy checks had a noticeable search cost.
This suggests solver3's current bottleneck is not simple immediate move reversal cycling.

description: Replaced simulated annealing with Late Acceptance Hill Climbing using a rolling 256-score history as the acceptance reference. This was a true non-SA driver, but it badly destabilized transfer attribute balance and large_gender, so the pure LAHC policy is not suitable in this form for solver3's canonical fixed-time bundle.

asi.hypothesis: A late-acceptance driver would escape SA's temperature-shape biases and improve basin traversal under fixed-time runs with modest overhead.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric regressed sharply to 106.4029, with especially bad damage on transfer attribute balance and large_gender, indicating that pure late-acceptance acceptance is too permissive or misaligned for solver3's penalty landscape.
asi.next_action_hint: Do not pursue pure LAHC unchanged. If revisiting non-SA acceptance, try a guarded variant: threshold acceptance with a shrinking cap, record-to-record travel, or a hybrid gate that only allows uphill moves when they stay within bounded regression on constraint-heavy penalties.
observations: Attribute-balance case worsened to 1.2525 fixed-time and 1.2794 fixed-iteration.
Large_gender worsened materially while sailing slightly improved versus reference.
Runtime overhead also increased to solver3_raw_score_us 36.07, so the driver was neither better nor cheaper.
asi.mechanism_family: non-SA acceptance / late acceptance hill climbing

description: Incubated the adaptive move-family bandit with a cost-aware UCB score that penalized expensive families by average family time. This variant catastrophically regressed the primary metric, largely by blowing up sailing, so this cost-aware bandit formulation is decisively not a keep.

asi.hypothesis: A cost-aware UCB family selector would preserve the upside of adaptive move-family allocation while avoiding over-spending on expensive families such as clique previews.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric worsened dramatically to 115.6401 and sailing exploded to 1.9590, indicating that the cost-aware bandit pushed solver3 into a very bad search regime.
asi.next_action_hint: Retire this cost-aware UCB variant. If online family adaptation is revisited, use richer reward signals tied to best-score improvements or case-agnostic structural conflict reduction, not simple improving-accept counts with cost penalties.
observations: The previous plain UCB family selector was mildly bad; adding cost awareness made the family much worse rather than better.
solver3_raw_score_us ballooned to 68.34 despite the intent to control cost.
The failure concentrated in sailing, suggesting the selector starved or mis-sequenced the move families that keep that landscape stable.

description: Replaced SA with a guarded non-SA record-to-record acceptance rule: always accept improving moves, but allow uphill moves only while they stay within a shrinking threshold above best-so-far. This beat the previous best fixed-time aggregate, improving sailing and transfer-attribute-balance materially while holding large_gender at reference.

asi.hypothesis: A guarded record-to-record acceptance driver would preserve useful uphill exploration without the instability of pure late acceptance, improving fixed-time objective quality on solver3's canonical suite.
asi.acceptance_rule: accept if candidate <= current_score or candidate <= best_score + threshold(progress)
tradeoffs: clique-swap functionality rose slightly above reference to 1.0019.
solver3_raw_score_us increased to 35.21 versus the 24.65 cooling-fix keep.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep this driver as the new baseline and incubate it with small guarded follow-ups: tune threshold schedule shape/scale, or combine it with a targeted structural neighborhood rather than broader family-selection changes.

description: Incubated the new record-to-record driver by tightening the threshold schedule to a quadratic decay (same initial threshold, faster close). This reduced raw score cost a bit, but it gave back too much objective quality versus the new 99.016 best, so the original linear schedule remains the better form.

asi.hypothesis: A faster-closing quadratic record-to-record threshold would retain the new driver's main gains while trimming late-run drift and recovering the synthetic/clique regressions.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric regressed from the new best 99.0160 to 99.6755, so the stricter quadratic closure gave up too much search quality despite slightly lower raw runtime cost.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the linear 2→0 record-to-record schedule as baseline. If continuing this family, tune around it locally (e.g. slightly different initial threshold or a small nonzero late floor) rather than closing the gate faster.
observations: solver3_raw_score_us improved from 35.21 to 33.21, but that efficiency gain did not translate into better fixed-time objective quality.
Sailing stayed strong, but transfer attribute balance worsened back above reference and the aggregate lost most of the keep's improvement margin.
This suggests the winning mechanism needs more late-stage freedom than the quadratic schedule allowed.

description: Tried a bigger architectural change: a GRASP-style multi-start outer driver that split each fixed-time run across several diverse constructor seeds and polished each start with the incumbent solver3 inner search, keeping the global best. It improved social_golfer, sailing, and synthetic coverage, but it badly regressed large_gender and transfer balance while almost doubling raw hotpath cost, so this naive equal-slice multi-start form is not competitive with the current single-trajectory best.

asi.hypothesis: Diversifying solver3 across several genuinely different constructor basins within the same fixed-time budget would outperform a single trajectory by finding better global regions, especially on broad-coverage cases.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric regressed from the current best 99.0160 to 100.9901. The outer multi-start spread the budget too thin and sacrificed constraint-heavy cases, especially large_gender and transfer attribute balance.
asi.next_action_hint: Do not reuse naive equal-slice multi-start as-is. If revisiting this family later, pair it with a much stronger constructor or an elite/portfolio policy that decides when an additional start is worth the budget.
observations: The family did show real basin-diversity effects: social_golfer, sailing, and synthetic all beat reference, which the current best does not do simultaneously.
The cost was substantial: solver3_raw_score_us rose to 47.61 from the current best's ~35 and well above the earlier 24.65 SA-fix baseline.
Large_gender and transfer-balance got materially worse, suggesting the baseline constructor does not produce enough high-quality starts for this outer-loop budget split to pay off.
This was a true big-search change, but the first naive form is not enough; the next big idea should avoid equal budget fragmentation without better structure.

description: First ALNS-style session ruin-and-recreate attempt crashed the canonical bundle. The targeted session rebuild operator could fail to place an active clique unit on stretch.sailing-trip-demo-real, and the optional macro-operator incorrectly surfaced that feasibility miss as a solver error instead of simply declining the perturbation.

asi.hypothesis: A targeted session-level ruin-and-recreate operator could escape stagnation by rebuilding one high-pressure session with a repeat-aware greedy repair while preserving immovables and clique integrity.
asi.rollback_reason: The experiment crashed on the canonical stretch suite because the rebuild operator propagated `session rebuild could not place unit of size 2 in session 0` as a hard solver error for sailing.
asi.next_action_hint: Re-run this family only after making the macro-operator fail-soft: impossible rebuild attempts must return no candidate rather than crashing the solver.
asi.error_detail: Constraint violation: session rebuild could not place unit of size 2 in session 0
asi.metrics_note: Crash run aborted before full metric aggregation; zeroed schema placeholder supplied only to satisfy logger requirements.

description: Incubated a larger ALNS-style session ruin-and-recreate operator with a fail-soft fallback: on prolonged stagnation, rebuild one high-pressure session using a repeat-aware greedy repair while preserving immovables and clique integrity. The mechanism avoided the crash and produced a respectable result near the old 100.105 baseline, but it still lost to the current 99.016 best and slightly worsened large_gender plus synthetic.

asi.hypothesis: A targeted session-level ruin-and-recreate macro move would let solver3 escape local minima without fragmenting the full fixed-time budget across separate starts.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric came in at 100.1118, which is close to the pre-99.016 baseline but still worse than the current best 99.0160. The macro operator did not produce a net quality gain large enough to keep.
asi.next_action_hint: Retire this naive session-level rebuild form for now. If revisiting ALNS later, use richer destroy targeting and a stronger repair heuristic instead of a simple whole-session repeat-aware greedy rebuild.
observations: The fail-soft repair made the operator safe, and the family can clearly move the search without exploding runtime.
Large_gender remained above reference and synthetic drifted further above reference, so the repair heuristic is not strong enough on structure-heavy cases.
This family looks more promising than naive equal-slice multi-start, but the first viable form is still not competitive with the current best.
asi.crash_followup: fixed infeasible rebuilds to return no candidate rather than hard-failing the solver

description: First memetic-style session crossover attempt crashed the canonical bundle. The macro operator generated a fresh donor schedule from an alternate constructor seed, but donor construction itself could fail on stretch.sailing-trip-demo-real, and that optional crossover path incorrectly surfaced the constructor failure as a hard solver error.

asi.hypothesis: Injecting one locally better donor session from a fresh schedule into the incumbent would provide GA-style recombination without fragmenting the run budget.
asi.rollback_reason: The experiment crashed because donor generation propagated `group 'Failboat' is full while placing immovable person 'person_aphix38e' in session 0 during solver3 normalization` as a hard solver error on sailing.
asi.next_action_hint: If this family is incubated, donor construction must be fail-soft: constructor failures should simply skip the crossover attempt rather than aborting the solve.
asi.error_detail: Constraint violation: group 'Failboat' is full while placing immovable person 'person_aphix38e' in session 0 during solver3 normalization
asi.metrics_note: Crash run aborted before full metric aggregation; zeroed schema placeholder supplied only to satisfy logger requirements.

description: Final incubation on the memetic family: instead of splicing only one donor session, copy the two most locally promising sessions from a fresh donor schedule into the incumbent during stagnation. This improved over the earlier one-session crossover on transfer balance, but it still did not beat the current 99.016 best and continued to pay a noticeable structural cost on large_gender.

asi.hypothesis: A slightly larger recombination block would capture more useful donor structure than a single-session splice while still preserving most of the incumbent schedule.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric improved relative to the first memetic crossover follow-up (99.62 vs 99.37? actually worse than 99.37? Wait correction: it remained worse than the best 99.016 and did not justify replacing the current champion), so the family remains a discard for now.
asi.next_action_hint: Retire this naive donor-session crossover family for now. If revisited later, combine donor selection with better structural compatibility checks or elite donor sources rather than simple constructor-seeded donors.
observations: This family was meaningfully stronger than naive multi-start and the simple ALNS repair, reaching 99.37 then 99.62 while preserving strong sailing and good transfer behavior.
The two-session splice did not improve on the one-session splice; larger crossover blocks from naive donors appear to disrupt large_gender structure.
Hotpath/runtime stayed much better than the earlier ALNS repair family, so recombination itself is not prohibitively expensive — the donor quality seems to be the main weakness.
asi.family_outcome: retire naive constructor-seeded donor crossover after two non-winning incubations

description: Incubated the highest-priority memetic idea as a bounded elite-donor burst: build a tiny diverse set of alternate constructor seeds, briefly polish them with the kept record-to-record driver, then try compatibility-aware 1-2 session splices back into the incumbent. This materially improved the memetic family over the old naive donor crossover (99.25 vs 99.37/99.62), but it still lost to the current 99.016 best.

asi.hypothesis: Polished diverse donors plus compatibility-aware session splicing would rescue the crossover family that previously failed with raw constructor-seeded donors.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric 99.2509 is directionally good but still worse than the kept 99.016 champion, so this first elite-burst memetic form is not yet a keep.
key_findings: This is the strongest memetic-family result so far after the kept baseline: 99.25 beats the earlier naive crossover follow-ups (99.37 and 99.62).
The run held large_gender exactly at reference and kept sailing strong (~0.956), so compatibility-aware donor selection reduced the structural damage seen in earlier crossover attempts.
Synthetic partial-attendance still drifted above reference (~1.012), which remains the main blocker alongside not quite clearing the 99.016 aggregate.
solver3_raw_score_us fell to ~20.94, so the bounded burst did not introduce catastrophic hotpath overhead.
asi.next_action_hint: If the memetic family gets another incubation turn, make it a true persistent elite population or allow selected offspring to re-enter search even when not immediately better than best-so-far; the current one-shot strict-improvement gate is probably leaving useful donor structure on the floor.

description: Memetic follow-up: same elite-donor burst, but instead of requiring the offspring to beat best-so-far immediately, allow a polished compatibility-aware splice to replace the current trajectory when it beats the current state or fits inside the active record-to-record band. This improved the first memetic burst again (99.17 vs 99.25), but it still did not beat the 99.016 champion.

asi.hypothesis: The first elite-donor burst was too strict by demanding an immediate new incumbent; letting good offspring continue the current trajectory should unlock follow-on search gains.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric improved versus the previous memetic burst but still landed at 99.1668, short of the kept 99.016 best.
key_findings: Relaxing the gate helped: this follow-up improved the previous elite-burst result from 99.25 to 99.17.
The gain came mostly from a better transfer-balance result (196 vs 197) while preserving the same strong sailing and large_gender behavior.
Fixed-iteration diagnostics were effectively unchanged, suggesting the memetic burst is helping fixed-time trajectory management more than raw basin quality.
solver3_raw_score_us fell again to ~20.16, so the bounded burst remains cheap.
asi.next_action_hint: If giving this family one more turn, the next step should be a persistent elite population across the run or a stronger synthetic-aware donor/session compatibility signal. If not, pivot to the freedom-aware GRASP constructor from the live queue.

description: First real persistent-population memetic run: keep a small elite pool of polished alternate-seed schedules across the solve, then on stagnation splice compatible donor sessions into the live trajectory and reinsert offspring into the pool. This materially changes the memetic architecture beyond the earlier one-shot bursts, but the first implementation regressed to 99.69 because large_gender drift outweighed strong fixed-iteration gains.

asi.hypothesis: A true persistent elite population will outperform bounded donor bursts by preserving useful donor structure across multiple macro recombination events instead of rebuilding elites from scratch each time.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric 99.6919 is well worse than the kept 99.016 champion, mainly from fixed-time large_gender regression to 1.025.
key_findings: This is a genuinely different memetic regime, not just another burst tweak: the population persists and offspring can re-enter the pool.

Fixed-iteration score improved below reference (99.78), with large_gender fixed-iteration also below reference, so the family may still be generating stronger basins than the fixed-time aggregate shows.

The fixed-time failure concentrated in large_gender while sailing and transfer balance remained good, suggesting the population trigger/integration is harming one case family rather than the whole search landscape.
Overhead stayed moderate in raw-score terms (~20.56 us), so quality integration rather than hotpath cost is the main issue.
asi.next_action_hint: One incubation follow-up is justified: make population activation more selective so macro recombination only fires for genuinely stuck runs/cases, or gate donor acceptance using a stronger compatibility test against large_gender-style structure. If that still misses, move the family to tried-and-retired and pivot to GRASP.

description: Tried a solver3-specific freedom-aware GRASP constructor that seeds each session by ranking clique/person placements with pair-repeat, forbidden-pair, pair-meeting, attribute-balance, should-together, and future-freedom slack heuristics before handing off to the kept record-to-record search. Checks passed, but fixed-time objective quality regressed badly versus the 99.016 champion, mainly because sailing-trip-demo-real worsened to 1.1387 normalized despite modest gains on several other cases.

asi.hypothesis: A solver3-native freedom-aware GRASP constructor would hand the record-to-record improver a much better initial incumbent by explicitly preferring low-repeat, feasible, attribute-aware, future-flexible placements.
asi.rollback_reason: The constructor produced broad case-level gains but fixed-time objective quality regressed sharply overall, with the sailing-trip case worsening enough to overwhelm the wins.
asi.next_action_hint: Back off from fully replacing the constructor; try injecting freedom-aware ideas as partial diversification, targeted restart seeding, or case-sensitive construction features instead of a universal constructor swap.
asi.learned: This heuristic family is directionally viable on social-golfer, gender-immovable, and attribute-balance cases, but it appears to overcommit early structure on sailing-trip-demo-real and fails as a one-size-fits-all initializer.

description: Tried a targeted ALNS/LNS-style restricted repair kernel inside solver3: on long stagnation, pick hotspot session/group pairs with repeated-contact pressure, lock immovables and active clique members, enumerate a capped exact reassignment over the remaining movable people across the two groups, and accept only strictly improving repairs before returning to record-to-record search. This was much stronger than the failed GRASP replacement and broadly helped sailing plus social_golfer, but it still missed the 99.016 champion because synthetic partial-attendance regressed to 1.0187 and large_gender only held at reference.

asi.hypothesis: A targeted two-group restricted repair kernel can make ALNS materially stronger than whole-session greedy rebuild by exact-searching a small hotspot neighborhood while preserving immovables and clique integrity.
asi.rollback_reason: The restricted repair kernel improved several important cases and got close to the champion, but it still lost on the primary metric because synthetic partial-attendance regressed and the remaining gap to 99.016 was not closed.
asi.next_action_hint: Incubate this ALNS family with smarter hotspot selection and repair scope: bias toward synthetic-capacity pressure, allow slightly richer subregions than one pair of groups, or use structural compatibility scoring to decide when the repair burst should fire.
asi.learned: This is the strongest fresh post-memetic direction so far. Two-group exact-ish repair helps sailing and social_golfer without blowing up runtime, but hotspot selection based mostly on repeat pressure is too narrow for synthetic-capacity-pressure cases.

description: Followed up the targeted ALNS/LNS repair family by making hotspot selection less repeat-only: rank candidate two-group repair regions using both repeated-contact pressure and the best immediately previewed swap/transfer delta inside the pair, then run the capped exact reassignment repair on the strongest regions. This slightly improved the first ALNS follow-up, especially by reducing the synthetic regression while keeping sailing strong, but it still did not beat the 99.016 champion.

asi.hypothesis: ALNS repair quality would improve if hotspot ranking used actual local move opportunity signals in addition to repeat pressure, better surfacing synthetic-capacity and balance-sensitive regions.
asi.rollback_reason: The smarter ranking improved the first ALNS follow-up a bit, especially on synthetic, but the family still fell short of the 99.016 champion.
asi.next_action_hint: One more ALNS incubation step could widen the repair neighborhood slightly when local preview potential is high, or add structural compatibility signatures to choose repair regions more selectively.
asi.learned: Local preview potential is a useful hotspot signal. The ALNS family is converging toward the champion without harming runtime, but two-group repairs still seem a bit too narrow to close the last gap.

description: Third ALNS incubation follow-up: after selecting the best targeted two-group repair region, allow one chained second-stage repair on an overlapping pair in the same session to broaden the neighborhood to an effectively three-group local region without exploding the exact repair kernel. This removed almost all of the synthetic regression, but it gave back some transfer/social-golfer quality and still did not beat the 99.016 champion.

asi.hypothesis: A chained second repair on an overlapping pair can widen the ALNS neighborhood just enough to fix synthetic-capacity issues without paying for a full three-group exact kernel.
asi.rollback_reason: The chained repair broadened the neighborhood but gave back quality on other cases and still stayed above the 99.016 champion.
asi.next_action_hint: ALNS may have reached diminishing returns in this two-group exact-repair family; either switch to a structural-signature region selector or pivot to the next fresh macro-level idea.
asi.learned: Broadening to an overlapping second pair reduces the synthetic miss, but the tradeoff curve suggests this exact family is close to saturated without a richer region model.
